CIS1908:160: William Cowper wrote this during a night watch. In December 1769 his dearest friend and caretaker, Mary Unwin — the woman whose household sheltered the fragile poet — fell dangerously ill, and Cowper, terrified of losing his human anchor, kept vigil. He recorded the hymn's birth himself in a letter: he began composing it before daybreak, in the dark hours of her illness, seeking the presence that could not be taken from him: O for a closer walk with God, a calm and heavenly frame, a light to shine upon the road that leads me to the Lamb. The hymn remembers better days with aching honesty — where is the blessedness I knew when first I saw the Lord? — and hunts the blocking idol with a surgeon's nerve: the dearest idol I have known, whate'er that idol be, help me to tear it from Thy throne. Mrs. Unwin recovered; Cowper's darkness would return in later years, as it always did. But the hymn stands as his steadiest prayer: so shall my walk be close with God, calm and serene my frame.
CIS1908:168: Cecil Frances Alexander had already taught the church's children most of their theology — "All Things Bright and Beautiful," "Once in Royal David's City," "There Is a Green Hill Far Away" were hers — when in 1852 she was asked for a hymn for St. Andrew's Day. She went to Andrew's story and found its hinge: a working fisherman, mid-task, hearing two words — follow Me. Her hymn sets that moment against the noise of every era: Jesus calls us o'er the tumult of our life's wild, restless sea; day by day His sweet voice soundeth, saying, 'Christian, follow Me.' The verses then do what Alexander always did — bring the lesson home from Galilee to the kitchen and the market: in our joys and in our sorrows, days of toil and hours of ease, still He calls, in cares and pleasures, 'Christian, love Me more than these.' The wife of an Irish archbishop, she spent her royalties on the sick and the deaf. She heard the call she wrote about, over her own tumult, and answered it in the plainest currency: obedience.
CIS1908:202: Lelia Morris wrote hymns at the kitchen sink and the camp meeting altar — an Ohio Methodist housewife who produced more than a thousand gospel songs, words and music together, this one in 1898: nearer, still nearer, close to Thy heart, draw me, my Savior, so precious Thou art. Then, in her early fifties, her sight began to fail. The family's answer has become one of gospel music's tenderest images: her son built her a blackboard twenty-eight feet long with oversized staff lines, so his mother could go on writing music she could barely see — and she did, composing hundreds more songs in the gathering dark. The hymn she had written with clear eyes became the testimony of her blindness: nearer, still nearer — the request of a woman whose world was closing in and whose Savior was not. Naught as an offering to Jesus I bring; only my sinful, now contrite heart. The church still sings her plea at altars, most singers never knowing about the blackboard — or that its author finished her life's work by faith, not by sight, in the most literal way possible.

CIS1908:265: Thomas Kelly was disinherited by respectability and repaid it with seven hundred hymns. Son of an Irish judge, bound for law, he was converted, took orders — and preached the gospel of grace so plainly that the Archbishop of Dublin banned him from the city's pulpits. Kelly preached on wherever doors opened, eventually building chapels of his own, and wrote hymns for sixty years, including "The Head That Once Was Crowned with Thorns" and this coronation shout of 1809: look, ye saints! the sight is glorious: see the Man of Sorrows now; from the fight returned victorious, every knee to Him shall bow. The hymn's engine is reversal — the Man of Sorrows now the crowned One, the mocking answered by coronation: crown Him! crown Him! crowns become the Victor's brow. Kelly, wealthy by inheritance, spent his fortune on Dublin's poor and was beloved by them. Banned from pulpits, he built his own; denied a hearing, he wrote hymns the whole church would sing. The reversal he preached, he lived — and his hymn still crowns the once-rejected King.
CIS1908:266: James Montgomery published this in his own newspaper on Christmas Eve. The editor of the Sheffield Iris — orphaned son of Moravian missionaries, twice jailed for his editorials, tireless enemy of the slave trade — set it before his readers on December 24, 1816: angels, from the realms of glory, wing your flight o'er all the earth; ye who sang creation's story, now proclaim Messiah's birth. The hymn is a summons list, each verse subpoenaing another group to the manger — angels, shepherds, sages, saints — with the same standing order for all: come and worship, come and worship, worship Christ, the newborn King. Montgomery knew about waiting for good news; his parents had died on the mission field, and his paper trafficked daily in the world's bad tidings. On the night before Christmas he used his front page for the oldest headline: the King is born. Newspapers yellow and perish; this piece of Christmas Eve journalism has run continuously for over two centuries, and its editor's summons still gathers the congregation it named.
CIS1908:267: William Chatterton Dix was a Bristol insurance manager, not a clergyman — and he wrote this hymn in bed. On the day the church read the story of the wise men, Dix lay seriously ill, unable to attend worship; he read Matthew 2 for himself, and by evening had written the hymn that walks in the magi's footprints: as with gladness men of old did the guiding star behold... so, most gracious Lord, may we evermore be led to Thee. The hymn's craft is its repeated hinge — as they did, so may we: as they knelt, so may we with willing feet ever seek the mercy seat; as they offered gifts most rare, so may we our costliest treasures bring. A sick layman turned his missed church service into one of the church's permanent possessions, proving something worth remembering: the Sabbath a believer cannot attend can still bear fruit, if the Book is open on the bed. There, it ends where the star pointed all along: where they need no star to guide, where no clouds Thy glory hide.
CIS1908:269: Francis Pott wrote this hymn for the sound of a machine being consecrated. In 1861, a church in Lancashire, England, was installing a new organ, and Pott — a scholarly translator-hymnist — supplied words for its dedication festival: angel voices, ever singing round Thy throne of light. The hymn asks the question every worship musician has quietly wondered: can it be that Thou regardest songs of sinful man? Can we know that Thou art near us, and wilt hear us? Yea, we can. From there it consecrates the whole apparatus of praise — craftsman's art and music's measure for Thy pleasure all combine — pipes, voices, hands, and skill offered like the magi's gifts: of Thy own gifts we give Thee. Written for one organ in one parish, it became the church's theology of church music itself: God does not need our sound, and delights in it anyway, the way a father regards a child's present bought with the father's own money. In Thy house, great God, we offer of Thine own to Thee — the hymn every choir and every hymnal-app maker gets to claim.
CIS1908:271: Edward Bickersteth heard a sermon on Isaiah 26:3 — Thou wilt keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on Thee — and that same Sunday in 1875 carried the text to the bedside of a dying relative. There, to comfort the sufferer, he wrote this hymn on the spot, in a form unlike any other in the book: every verse is a question and its answer. Peace, perfect peace, in this dark world of sin? The blood of Jesus whispers peace within. Peace, perfect peace, with sorrows surging round? On Jesus' bosom naught but calm is found. Verse by verse the hymn interrogates every peace-thief — loved ones far away, an unknown future, death itself — and each time the answer holds: Jesus we know, and He is on the throne. Written in a sickroom in an afternoon, it has returned to sickrooms ever since, doing its original work: it does not deny the dark world, the surging sorrows, or the shadow; it out-answers them, one whispered verse at a time.
CIS1908:299: William Featherston was a Montreal teenager when he wrote one of the church's great love songs — by the common account, around age sixteen, apparently as a response to his own conversion: my Jesus, I love Thee, I know Thou art mine; for Thee all the follies of sin I resign. He sent the poem to an aunt, who preserved it; it found its way into print, and years later the Boston musician Adoniram Gordon gave it the melody the world sings. Featherston himself died at about twenty-six, young enough that the hymn's third verse reads like unintended prophecy: I'll love Thee in life, I will love Thee in death, and praise Thee as long as Thou lendest me breath. The hymn's structure is a love that keeps its tenses straight — I love Thee because Thou hast first loved me — and its last verse reaches where its young author went early: in mansions of glory and endless delight, I'll ever adore Thee in heaven so bright. A boy wrote it, a short life sealed it, and the church has been borrowing his vow ever since: if ever I loved Thee, my Jesus, 'tis now.

CIS1908:335: Robert Grant governed an empire's province and knew exactly how small that made him. A member of Parliament who fought to open England's legislature to Jews, and later Governor of Bombay, Grant wrote this in the 1830s from the old 104th Psalm: O worship the King, all glorious above, and gratefully sing His power and His love. The hymn is court language written by a man who had seen real courts — pavilioned in splendor, and girded with praise — and its genius is the descent it makes: from the canopy of space and the chariots of wrath down to the singer's own smallness: frail children of dust, and feeble as frail, in Thee do we trust, nor find Thee to fail. A colonial governor confessing himself dust, in meter, is its own sermon. Grant died in India in 1838; his brother published his hymns the next year. The empire he served has dissolved; the King he worshiped has not — our Maker, Defender, Redeemer, and Friend — and Grant's psalm still files its accurate report on where power actually lives.

CIS1908:359: Horatius Bonar wrote this for children — bored ones. Serving in Leith and Kelso, Scotland, the young minister noticed his Sunday school children droning the psalm versions without their hearts, and began writing hymns they could feel, fitting new words to tunes they loved. From that workshop came what many judge the finest Scottish hymn of its century: I heard the voice of Jesus say, 'Come unto Me and rest.' Its architecture is a perfect teaching pattern — each verse half invitation, half testimony: Jesus speaks (come... drink... look), and the singer reports what happened: I came to Jesus as I was, weary and worn and sad; I found in Him a resting place, and He has made me glad. Three invitations, three findings — rest, living water, light — a child's catechism of experience. Bonar wrote some six hundred hymns and never chose one as his favorite; the church chose for him. What began as a cure for Sunday school boredom became every generation's plainest account of what actually happens when the Voice is taken at its word: and in that light of life I'll walk till traveling days are done.

CIS1908:361: Here is a hymn with a special handshake for Sabbath keepers: its author kept the seventh day. Samuel Stennett was pastor of a Seventh Day Baptist congregation in London in the 1700s — heir to a family line of Sabbath-keeping ministers — and one of the most respected dissenting preachers of his age. From his pen came this meditation on the loveliness of Christ, drawn from the Song of Songs and Psalm 45: majestic sweetness sits enthroned upon the Savior's brow; His head with radiant glories crowned, His lips with grace o'erflow. The hymn's centerpiece is personal accounting: to Him I owe my life and breath, and all the joys I have; He makes me triumph over death, and saves me from the grave. Stennett — who also wrote "On Jordan's Stormy Banks" — declined worldly advancement to stay with his congregation. Centuries before the Adventist movement arose, a Sabbath-keeping pastor was already singing the seventh-day faith's favorite theme: the altogether lovely One, seen clearly enough to make every rival glory fade.
CIS1908:375: Folliott Pierpoint was a young Bath schoolmaster in his late twenties when, as the account is commonly given, the beauty of the countryside around his city in late spring moved him to write a thanksgiving in 1864: for the beauty of the earth, for the glory of the skies. He meant it for communion — its refrain originally offered the sacrifice of praise in eucharistic language — but the hymn escaped the service and became the church's all-purpose gratitude list. Its method is an inventory that keeps widening: sun and moon, hill and vale, then inward to the senses — for the joy of ear and eye, for the heart and mind's delight — then outward to the loves that make life dear: brother, sister, parent, child; friends on earth, and friends above. By the end, the singer has counted past counting and lands where counting always should: for Thy church that evermore lifteth holy hands above. Pierpoint lived quietly and long; his one famous morning of gratitude has supplied words for a century and a half of harvest festivals, weddings, and ordinary Sabbaths when the world simply looked well-made.
CIS1908:458: John Greenleaf Whittier was a Quaker, and Quakers historically did not sing in worship — which makes him one of hymnody's loveliest accidents. The beloved American poet, lifelong abolitionist who was mobbed and stoned for the cause, wrote long devotional poems, and the church quarried its hymns from them without asking. This one comes from his poem "Our Master": we may not climb the heavenly steeps to bring the Lord Christ down; in vain we search the lowest deeps, for Him no depths can drown. The verses are Romans 10 in Quaker plainness — no ascent required, no descent possible, for the Word is near: but warm, sweet, tender, even yet a present help is He; and faith has still its Olivet, and love its Galilee. The healing of His seamless dress is by our beds of pain — Whittier's Christ is findable exactly where people actually are: bedsides, ordinary rooms, the present tense. A man whose tradition kept silence in meeting gave the singing church some of its quietest, surest lines: the sound of the gentle stillness, set to music he never intended.
CIS1908:618: Mary Duncan wrote this for her own two babies, and she never saw them grow up. A young Scottish minister's wife, she composed little verses for her children's evening prayers in the closing months of 1839 — Jesus, tender Shepherd, hear me; bless Thy little lamb tonight; through the darkness be Thou near me, keep me safe till morning light. Within weeks, in January 1840, she died suddenly at thirty-five — some accounts say of a chill that turned fatal. Her husband gathered her verses and published them in her memoir; the bedtime prayer she had made for two small children in a Scottish manse became the bedtime prayer of the English-speaking world. There is a completeness to its three short verses that no editor has improved: safety asked for the night, gratitude for the day, and the wider family remembered — let my sins be all forgiven, bless the friends I love so well. Generations of children, taught it by mothers who never knew its author, have prayed a young mother's last gift without knowing it was one. The tender Shepherd she trusted her lambs to has kept the appointment nightly ever since.
CIS1908:729: This beloved children's hymn keeps a secret on its byline: no one is certain who wrote it. It appeared in 1836 in a collection compiled by Dorothy Ann Thrupp — "Hymns for the Young" — unsigned, as her practice often was; because she edited the book, tradition credits her, but the attribution has never been proved, and honest hymnals print a question mark. We say so plainly: the church's classic prayer for shepherding was written by someone who declined to be identified. Perhaps that is fitting. Savior, like a shepherd lead us; much we need Thy tender care — the hymn's whole posture is dependence, not display: in Thy pleasant pastures feed us, for our use Thy folds prepare. Blessed Jesus, Thou hast bought us, Thine we are. William Bradbury — the American who gave "Jesus Loves Me" its tune — set it to the melody that carried it around the world. Sung at children's divisions, baptisms, and funerals alike, it remains the prayer of the flock that does not need to know the undershepherd's name, because it knows the Shepherd's: early let us seek Thy favor, early let us do Thy will.

CIS1908:779: William Cullen Bryant was America's first celebrated poet — the man who wrote "Thanatopsis" as a teenager and edited the New York Evening Post for half a century, wielding it against slavery. This hymn is quarried from his 1839 poem "The Battle-Field," and one of its stanzas became proverbial in the mouths of reformers and was quoted for a century in freedom's causes: truth, crushed to earth, shall rise again; the eternal years of God are hers; but error, wounded, writhes in pain, and dies among his worshipers. Bryant wrote of moral battle, not muskets — the hymn honors those who fight the unpopular long fight: they never fail who die in a great cause. Early Adventists, a small people holding unfashionable truths against the current, loved lines like these for good reason: they state the arithmetic of every minority that happens to be right. Time is on truth's side because eternity is. The poet-editor spent his life betting his newspaper on that arithmetic; the hymn preserves the wager in verse, and history keeps paying it out.
CIS1908:822: Frances Havergal wrote this as a New Year's card. For January 1874 she had a greeting printed to send her friends — a small card bearing a fresh poem: another year is dawning, dear Father, let it be, in working or in waiting, another year with Thee. The card's verses walked the unknown year ahead and pre-assigned every part of it — another year of progress, another year of praise; another year of leaning upon Thy loving breast; of service, of training — before sealing the whole calendar with one address: another year for Thee. It was, in effect, a consecration disguised as stationery. Havergal had only five more new years to live, and she spent them exactly as the card proposed. The church adopted the greeting as its watch-night and New Year hymn, which means that every January, congregations around the world still receive Frances Havergal's card, a century and a half late and perfectly on time — the year ahead handed over before it starts, on earth, or else in heaven, another year for Thee.
CIS1908:842: Thomas Rawson Taylor was preparing for the pulpit when his body failed him. An English student of theology, he preached briefly, but consumption ended his ministry almost as it began, and he died in 1835 at twenty-seven. From his sickroom came the hymn that turned his shortened lease into theology: I'm but a stranger here; heaven is my home. Earth is a desert drear; heaven is my home. The verses face his facts without flinching — danger and sorrow stand round me on every hand — and then file the change of address that made them bearable: heaven is my fatherland, heaven is my home. There is no self-pity in it; there is itinerary. And time's wild wintry blast soon shall be overpast; I shall reach home at last. The young man who never got his pulpit has preached from this hymn for nearly two centuries, to every believer who has felt the lease shortening — the sermon a dying student left behind: hold the present loosely; you are traveling.
CIS1908:95: John Stockton was a Methodist gospel-song evangelist whose health repeatedly broke and whose songs kept working when he could not. He wrote this invitation hymn — come, every soul by sin oppressed, there's mercy with the Lord — and the account passed down from the Moody-Sankey campaigns says the refrain owes its final form to Ira Sankey, who, preparing the song for the great meetings, felt the chorus should say more plainly what the seeker must do, and shaped it to the words the world knows: only trust Him, only trust Him, only trust Him now; He will save you, He will save you, He will save you now. The detail rests on Sankey's own recollections, and we tell it as his telling. Stockton died in 1877, mid-revival season; his hymn kept the appointment without him. Its arithmetic is the gospel's shortest: for Jesus shed His precious blood rich blessings to bestow — therefore, one act, stated three times so no one misses it. Plunge now into the crimson flood that washes white as snow: an invitation built to be understood on first hearing, which is exactly what invitations are for.

CIS1908:329: Joseph Addison published this in The Spectator in 1712, at the close of an essay on the right uses of wonder — the great essayist arguing that the surest cure for atheism is simply looking up. The poem sets Psalm 19 in the language of reason's own age: the spacious firmament on high, with all the blue ethereal sky, and spangled heavens, a shining frame, their great Original proclaim. Its boldest move is the third verse's admission: the stars are silent. What though in solemn silence all move round the dark terrestrial ball? No real voice nor sound amid the radiant orbs be found — and then the turn that answers every era's skepticism: in reason's ear they all rejoice... forever singing as they shine, 'The hand that made us is divine.' Haydn's majestic music from The Creation later became its wings. Addison wrote for coffee-house readers who prided themselves on thinking; his hymn grants the premise and wins the argument: think, then — and the silence of the stars becomes the loudest testimony in the sky.
CIS1908:80: This book — Christ in Song itself — is one man's life work, and his story deserves telling with both honor and honesty. Franklin Edson Belden (1858–1945) was born into the very heart of the young Advent movement: raised in Battle Creek, the son of Sarah Harmon Belden — sister of Ellen White — which made him the prophet's nephew and a child of the movement's founding family. He grew into Adventism's songwriter. His gift was startling: words and music often came to him together, and it was said he could hear a sermon in the morning and have a finished song ready for the congregation by the afternoon — melody, harmony, and verses complete. Camp meetings watched him do it repeatedly. He poured that gift into the church's books: contributions to Hymns and Tunes in 1886, Sabbath school songbooks, and then his masterwork — Christ in Song, first issued in 1900 and enlarged in 1908 into the collection of nearly a thousand pieces this shelf preserves. His own hymns thread through it: "We Know Not the Hour," "Cover with His Life" ("Look upon Jesus, sinless is He"), "I Will Sing of Jesus' Love," "Ask Not to Be Excused," and many more. Then comes the sorrow, and we tell it plainly: a dispute over the royalties and rights to Christ in Song opened a breach between Belden and the church's leadership in the early 1900s, and it never fully healed. He spent his last decades estranged from the denomination his songs had helped to build, dying in Battle Creek in 1945. The church kept singing his music through it all — and sings it still. Perhaps that is the gentlest verdict history offers: the quarrel belonged to time, and the songs did not. Every hymn in this book passed through his hands; whoever sings from Christ in Song is, in a real sense, singing with Belden — and with the Christ he never stopped putting first in the title.

Camp meetings watched him do it repeatedly. He poured that gift into the church's books: contributions to Hymns and Tunes in 1886, Sabbath school songbooks, and then his masterwork — Christ in Song, first issued in 1900 and enlarged in 1908 into the collection of nearly a thousand pieces this shelf preserves. His own hymns thread through it: "We Know Not the Hour," "Cover with His Life" ("Look upon Jesus, sinless is He"), "I Will Sing of Jesus' Love," "Ask Not to Be Excused," and many more. Then comes the sorrow, and we tell it plainly: a dispute over the royalties and rights to Christ in Song opened a breach between Belden and the church's leadership in the early 1900s, and it never fully healed. He spent his last decades estranged from the denomination his songs had helped to build, dying in Battle Creek in 1945. The church kept singing his music through it all — and sings it still. Perhaps that is the gentlest verdict history offers: the quarrel belonged to time, and the songs did not. Every hymn in this book passed through his hands; whoever sings from Christ in Song is, in a real sense, singing with Belden — and with the Christ he never stopped putting first in the title.
